title: Computer Architecture and Organization: von Neumann Model, Memory, Cache Introduction
key_concepts: data bus / address bus / control bus

definitions.description: A computer where program instructions are stored in the same memory as data; instructions are executed sequentially (or with branches), with a PC tracking the next instruction.
The abstract interface between software and hardware: defines the instruction set, registers, and addressing modes visible to the programmer.
A program that translates assembly language into machine language.
A program that translates a high-level language into machine language (directly or via assembly language).
Non-volatile memory whose contents can be read but not (easily) modified.
Volatile read/write memory; loses its contents when power is removed.
High-speed, high-price RAM; retains data as long as powered.
Slower, cheaper, denser RAM; requires periodic refresh.
The most significant byte of a multi-byte value is stored at the lowest memory address.
The least significant byte of a multi-byte value is stored at the lowest memory address.
Layered memory organisation with increasing capacity and decreasing speed: registers → cache → main memory → disk.

examples.name: Byte order example
examples.description: unsigned int x = 0xB67A49E3: big-endian stores B6 at address 153; little-endian stores E3 at address 153.

examples.name: Address decoding example
examples.description: 32-bit processor with four 1 GB memory circuits: 2 address bits select the circuit; partial decoding creates aliased addresses.

learning_objectives: Describe the von Neumann computer model
Explain the roles of assembler and compiler in language translation
List the main hardware components of a computing system
Understand memory structure and address decoding
Explain big-endian and little-endian byte ordering
Describe the memory hierarchy and the motivation for cache memory
